mission.title: Install and Configure Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE)
mission.description: Linode Kubernetes Engine (LKE) is a managed Kubernetes service that simplifies the deployment and management of Kubernetes clusters on Linode's infrastructure. It is ideal for users looking for a hassle-free way to run Kubernetes without managing the underlying hardware. Key trade-offs include limited customization compared to self-managed clusters and potential higher costs for managed services.

steps.title: Install the Linode CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the Linode CLI, which is necessary for managing Linode resources from the command line.

```bash

curl -sL https://github.com/linode/linode-cli/releases/latest/download/linode-cli-linux-amd64 -o linode-cli && chmod +x linode-cli && sudo mv linode-cli /usr/local/bin/

```

> This command downloads the latest Linode CLI binary and makes it executable.
commands: curl -sL https://github.com/linode/linode-cli/releases/latest/download/linode-cli-linux-amd64 -o linode-cli && chmod +x linode-cli && sudo mv linode-cli /usr/local/bin/

steps.title: Create a Kubernetes Cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Kubernetes cluster on Linode with the specified version, node count, and region.

```bash

linode-cli lke cluster-create --label my-cluster --region us-east --k8s-version 1.30 --node-count 3

```

> Replace 'my-cluster' with your desired cluster name.

steps.title: Configure Kubeconfig
steps.description:
This step sets up your kubeconfig file to access the newly created Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

linode-cli lke kubeconfig-view my-cluster --format yaml > ~/.kube/config

```

> This command fetches the kubeconfig for your cluster and saves it to the default kubeconfig location.

steps.title: Verify Cluster Nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your Kubernetes cluster to ensure they are running.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see all nodes in 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
This step involves setting up autoscaling, monitoring, and RBAC for your cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/linode/linode-cloud-controller-manager/v0.9.6/deploy/ccm-linode-template.yaml

```

> This command deploys the Linode Cloud Controller Manager.

uninstall.title: Delete the Kubernetes Cluster
uninstall.description:
This command deletes the Kubernetes cluster and all associated resources, which may result in data loss.

```bash

linode-cli lke cluster-delete my-cluster

```

> Make sure to backup any important data before running this command.

uninstall.title: Remove the Linode CLI
uninstall.description:
This command removes the Linode CLI from your system.

```bash

sudo rm /usr/local/bin/linode-cli

```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Kubernetes Cluster
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you backup your data and drain the nodes to prevent disruptions.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

linode-cli lke cluster-update my-cluster --k8s-version 1.30

kubectl uncordon <node-name>

```

> Replace <node-name> with the name of the node you want to drain.
> This command upgrades your cluster to the specified version.
> Uncordon the node after the upgrade is complete.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates an issue with the kubeconfig file or the cluster not being reachable.

**Fix:**
Regenerate the kubeconfig using 'linode-cli lke kubeconfig-view my-cluster --format yaml > ~/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'No resources found in default namespace'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the cluster is not properly initialized or if resources are not deployed.

**Fix:**
Check the status of your nodes with 'kubectl get nodes' and ensure they are in 'Ready' state.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create service: Service not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the service you are trying to access does not exist.

**Fix:**
Verify the service name and check its status with 'kubectl get services'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that your cluster does not have enough resources to schedule the pods.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster by adding more nodes or upgrading existing nodes.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for new Kubernetes features such as PodSecurity admission and improved scheduling capabilities.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy APIs that are no longer recommended for use.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: Ensure that all existing resources are compatible with the new API versions before upgrading.

resolution.summary: You should see output similar to 'NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION' with all nodes listed as 'Ready'.
codeSnippets: curl -sL https://github.com/linode/linode-cli/releases/latest/download/linode-cli-linux-amd64 -o linode-cli && chmod +x linode-cli && sudo mv linode-cli /usr/local/bin/

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the Linode CLI installed and configured with your API token.
